subject: Physics
q: The point A moves with a uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius 0.36 m and covers 30° in 0.1 s. The perpendicular projection P from A on the diameter MN represents the simple harmonic motion of P. The restoration force per unit mass when P touches M will be :
question: The point A moves with a uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius 0.36 m and covers 30° in 0.1 s. The perpendicular projection P from A on the diameter MN represents the simple harmonic motion of P. The restoration force per unit mass when P touches M will be :
solution:
The point A covers 30° in 0.1 s.
Then, we have π6 rad⟶0.1 s.
Or 1 rad⟶0.1π6 s
Or 2π in→ 0.1×6π×2π
Thus, time taken is T=1.2 sec.

We know that ω=2πT
Then, ω=2π1.2
Restoration force (F)=mω2A.

Then Restoration force per unit mass Fm=ω2 A
Fm=2π1.22×0.36

≅9.87 N

subject: Physics
q: The general displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> be its time period. The slope of its potential energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>) – time (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>) curve will be maximum when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>T</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is ______.
question: The general displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> be its time period. The slope of its potential energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>) – time (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>) curve will be maximum when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>T</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Given here, displacement x=A sin ωt

Now, potential energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is U=12mω2x2=12mω2A2sin2ωt,

So, slope of potential energy and time curve is dUdt=12mω3A22sin2ωt

Now, dUdtmax will be maximum when sin2ωt=1

 ⇒2ωt=π2⇒22πTt=π2⇒t=T8⇒β=8

q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion. Its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and time period is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The time it will take to travel from its position of maximum displacement to the point corresponding to half of its amplitude, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>
question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion. Its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and time period is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The time it will take to travel from its position of maximum displacement to the point corresponding to half of its amplitude, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>
solution:
Given here, A=8 cm and T=6 s

The displacement equation in SHM, when amplitude is halved is Acos2πtT=A2

⇒2πtT=π3

Time taken by the particle is t= T6=66=1sec.

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a spring is kept in a stretched position with some extension by holding the masses 1 kg and 0.2 kg with a separation more than spring natural length and are released. Assuming the horizontal surface to be frictionless, the angular frequency (in SI unit) of the system is : $k=150 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
question: As shown in the figure, a spring is kept in a stretched position with some extension by holding the masses 1 kg and 0.2 kg with a separation more than spring natural length and are released. Assuming the horizontal surface to be frictionless, the angular frequency (in SI unit) of the system is : $k=150 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
solution:
Two masses (1 kg and 0.2 kg) connected by a spring (k = 150 N/m) on a frictionless surface undergo oscillation about their center of mass.
This two-body problem reduces to SHM with reduced mass $\mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{1 \times 0.2}{1.2} = \frac{1}{6}$ kg.
The angular frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{150}{1/6}} = \sqrt{900} = 30$ rad/s.

subject: Physics
q: A simple harmonic oscillator of angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is acted upon by an external force <math><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi> </mi><mi>N</mi><mo>. </mo></mrow></mrow></math>If the oscillator is at rest in its equilibrium position at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> its position at later times is proportional to:
question: A simple harmonic oscillator of angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is acted upon by an external force <math><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi> </mi><mi>N</mi><mo>. </mo></mrow></mrow></math>If the oscillator is at rest in its equilibrium position at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> its position at later times is proportional to:
solution:
It is given that oscillator at rest at t=0 i.e. at t=0, v=0. So, in option, we can check by putting v=dxdt=0.

(1) If x∝sin⁡t+12cos⁡2t

⟹v∝cos⁡t+12×2 (-sin⁡2t)

⟹  at t=0, v∝1-0≠0

(2) If x ∝cos⁡t-12sin⁡t

⟹  v∝-sin⁡t-12cos⁡t

⟹at t=0, v∝-12≠0

(3) If x∝sin⁡t-12 sin 2t

then υ∝cos⁡t-12×2cos⁡2t

⟹at t=0, v∝1-1=0

(4) If x∝sin⁡t+12sin⁡2t

⟹v∝cos⁡t+12×2cos⁡2t

⟹at t=0, v∝1+1

⟹  v∝2≠0

∴   in option 3  v=0   at   t=0

subject: Physics
q: In the figure given below. a block of mass M=490 g placed on a frictionless table is connected with two springs having same spring constant (K=2 N m-1). If the block is horizontally displaced through X m then the number of complete oscillations it will make in 14π seconds will be ______.
question: In the figure given below. a block of mass M=490 g placed on a frictionless table is connected with two springs having same spring constant (K=2 N m-1). If the block is horizontally displaced through X m then the number of complete oscillations it will make in 14π seconds will be ______.
solution:
Both the springs are connected in parallel, therefore

Keq=K+K=2K

Now, time period of spring-block system is given by,

T=2πmKeq=2πm2K

Given here, m=490 gm=0.49 kg and K=2 N m-1

So, T=2π0.492×2

=2π49400=2π720=7π10

Now, number of oscillation in 14π is

N=timeT=14π7π10=20

q: The amplitude of a mass-spring system, which is executing simple harmonic motion decreases with time. If mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, Decay constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then how much time is required for the amplitude of the system to drop to half of its initial value? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The amplitude of a mass-spring system, which is executing simple harmonic motion decreases with time. If mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, Decay constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then how much time is required for the amplitude of the system to drop to half of its initial value? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
A=A0e-γt=A0e-bt2m

A02=A0e-bt2m

bt2m=ln2

t=2mbln2=2×500×0.69320

t=34.65 second.

q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion represented by displacement function as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. If the position and velocity of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively, then its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion represented by displacement function as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. If the position and velocity of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively, then its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
x(t)=Asin(ωt+ϕ)

v(t)=Aωcos(ωt+ϕ)

2=Asinϕ ...1

2ω=Aωcosϕ  ...2

From (1) and (2)

tanϕ=1

ϕ=45°

Putting the value of ϕ in equation (1)

2=A12

A=22

x=2

At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, it is at its position of equilibrium. The kinetic energy - time graph of the particle will look like:
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with a time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, it is at its position of equilibrium. The kinetic energy - time graph of the particle will look like:
solution:
x=Asin⁡ω t

v=dxdt=a ωcos⁡ω t

K.E.=12m v2

=12 m A2ω2cos2⁡ ωt

=12 m A2ω2cos2⁡2πT t

At t=0, K.E. is maximum.

The period of K.E. graph is half of the period of displacement graph.

Comment:- The points on the graph of x-axis should be differentiable.

In all option mentioned, the graph has cusps at the points on x-axis.

q: The potential energy of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> in motion along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The time period of the particle for small oscillation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>≃</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is _____ .
question: The potential energy of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> in motion along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The time period of the particle for small oscillation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>≃</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Given here, potential energy, U=41-cos4x J

Using the relation between conservative force and potential energy, F=-dUdx.

We have, F=-4+sin4x4=-16sin4x

For small θ, sinθ≈θ.

Acceleration of particle is a=-64xm=-64x4=-16x

As the oscillations are simple harmonic in nature, so  a=-ω2x⇒ω2=16⇒ω=4 rad s-1

Now, time period of oscillation isT=2πω=π2.

Thus, the value of K=2.

question: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
solution:
In case of SHM in spring-block system, angular frequency is given by, ω=km.

Therefore,

ω2ω1=m1m2=12

q: The total energy of particle, executing simple harmonic motion is
question: The total energy of particle, executing simple harmonic motion is
solution: In simple harmonic motion, as a particle is displaced from its mean position, its kinetic energy is converted to potential energy and vice versa and total energy remains constant. The total energy of simple harmonic motion is independent of $x$.

Ta=2πMK

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> executes a simple harmonic motion under a periodic force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The particle attains a maximum speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> during its oscillation. The amplitude of the motion is ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> executes a simple harmonic motion under a periodic force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The particle attains a maximum speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> during its oscillation. The amplitude of the motion is ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Given, force F=(–25x) N

⇒0.25a=-25x

⇒a=-100x

Now comparing it with standard equation, a=-ω2x we get

ω=10.

Now, vmax=ωA

⇒ωA=4

⇒A=410=0.4 m

⇒A=40 cm.

subject: Physics
q: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> attached to free end of a spring executes SHM with a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the mass is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> the period of oscillation increases by one second, the value of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>.
question: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> attached to free end of a spring executes SHM with a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the mass is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> the period of oscillation increases by one second, the value of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>.
solution:
Time period of oscillation is given by T=2πmk, where k is the force constant of the spring. 

∵ Spring is same, therefore, spring constant will be same.

Putting the different values,

In the first case          T=1=2πmk

In the second case    T'=2=2πm+3k

 Dividing the equations, we get

 TT'=12=mm+3

⇒mm+3=14⇒4m=m+3⇒m=1

Thus, m=1 kg

q: For a simple pendulum, a graph is plotted between its kinetic energy (K.E.) and potential energy (P.E.) against its displacement d. which one of the following represents these correctly? (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question: For a simple pendulum, a graph is plotted between its kinetic energy (K.E.) and potential energy (P.E.) against its displacement d. which one of the following represents these correctly? (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
For simple pendulum performing the simple harmonic motion, displacement, y=Asinωtv=dydt=ωAcosωt=ωA2-y2⇒K.E.=12mv2, 
This represents a downward parabola, Y=±A, K.E.=12ω2m A2-A2=0. P.E.=12mω2y2. This represents an upward parabola. On plotting graphs of potential energy & kinetic energy.

If pressed downward slightly and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion at angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of the bottle is <math><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is close to: <math><mo>(</mo></math> density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>)</mo></math>
question: A cylindrical plastic bottle of negligible mass is filled with <math><mn>310</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>m</mi><mi>l</mi></math> of water and left floating in a pond with still water. If pressed downward slightly and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion at angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of the bottle is <math><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is close to: <math><mo>(</mo></math> density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Let at equilibrium, y length of bottle is immerged in water:

So, mg=fbuo

mg=ρAyg ..... (1) [Equilibrium Condition]

Now it is further pushed by distance x

Frestoring=ρ[Ax+y)g-mg

mω2x=ρAxg+ρAyg-mg

ω=ρAgm=ρAgρV=πr2gV=7.9 rad/sec

subject: Physics
q:
A simple pendulum is placed at a place where its distance from the earth's surface is equal to the radius of the earth. If the length of the string is 4 m, then the time period of small oscillations will be _________s.

[take g=π2 m s-2]
question:
A simple pendulum is placed at a place where its distance from the earth's surface is equal to the radius of the earth. If the length of the string is 4 m, then the time period of small oscillations will be _________s.

[take g=π2 m s-2]
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at surface of earth, g=GMR2.

Now, at distance R from the surface of the earth, g'=GMR+R2=g4

Therefore,
T=2πlg4

⇒T=2π4×4 g

⇒T=2π4π=8 s

subject: Physics
q: In the reported figure, two bodies A and B of masses 200 g and 800 g are attached with the system of springs. Springs are kept in a stretched position with some extension when the system is released. The horizontal surface is assumed to be frictionless. The angular frequency will be _______  rad s-1 when k=20 N m-1.
question: In the reported figure, two bodies A and B of masses 200 g and 800 g are attached with the system of springs. Springs are kept in a stretched position with some extension when the system is released. The horizontal surface is assumed to be frictionless. The angular frequency will be _______  rad s-1 when k=20 N m-1.
solution:
ω=keqμ

μ= reduced mass

Springs are in series connection, 

keq=k1k2k1+k2

keq=k×4k5k=4k5

keq=4×205 N m-1=16 N m-1

μ=m1 m2 m1+m2=0.2×0.80.2+0.8=0.16 kg

ω=160.16=100=10

subject: Physics
q: If a simple pendulum has significant amplitude (up to a factor of $1 / e$ of original) only in the period between $t=0s$ to $t=\tau s$, then $\tau$ may be called the average life of the pendulum. When the spherical bob of the pendulum suffers a retardation (due to viscous drag) proportional to its velocity, with '$b$' as the constant of proportionality, the average life time of the pendulum is (assuming damping is small) in seconds:
question: If a simple pendulum has significant amplitude (up to a factor of $1 / e$ of original) only in the period between $t=0s$ to $t=\tau s$, then $\tau$ may be called the average life of the pendulum. When the spherical bob of the pendulum suffers a retardation (due to viscous drag) proportional to its velocity, with '$b$' as the constant of proportionality, the average life time of the pendulum is (assuming damping is small) in seconds:
solution: As retardation $=\mathrm{bv}$ $\therefore$ retarding force $=\mathrm{mbv}$ $\therefore$ net restoring torque when angular displacement is $\theta$ is given by $=-\operatorname{mg} \ell \sin \theta+\operatorname{mbv} \ell$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{I} \alpha=-m g \ell \sin \theta+\operatorname{mbv} \ell$ where, $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{m} \ell^2$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \theta}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=\alpha=-\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell} \sin \theta+\frac{\mathrm{bv}}{\ell}$ for small damping, the solution of the above differential equation will be $\therefore \quad \theta=\theta_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{\mathrm{bt}}{2}} \sin (w t+\phi)$ $\therefore \quad$ angular amplitude will be $=\theta . e^{\frac{-b t}{2}}$ According to question, in $\tau$ time (average life-time), angular amplitude drops to $\frac{1}{e}$ value of its original value $(\theta)$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\theta_0}{\mathrm{e}}=\theta_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{6 \tau}{2}}$ $\frac{6 \tau}{2}=1$ $\therefore \quad \tau=\frac{2}{b}$

q: The displacement of a damped harmonic oscillator is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">φ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Here <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in seconds. The time taken for its amplitude of vibration to drop to half of its initial value is close to:
question: The displacement of a damped harmonic oscillator is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">φ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Here <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in seconds. The time taken for its amplitude of vibration to drop to half of its initial value is close to:
solution:
Amplitude A=A0e-kt
From given condition, A02=A0e-0.1×t
⇒ln2=0.1×t
t=ln20.1=0.6930.10=6.935≈7 s

q: For what value of displacement the kinetic energy and potential energy of a simple harmonic oscillation become equal?
question: For what value of displacement the kinetic energy and potential energy of a simple harmonic oscillation become equal?
solution:
KE=PE

12mω2A2-x2=12mω2x2

A2-x2=x2

2x2=A2

x=±A2

question: The displacement $y(t)=A \sin (\omega t+\phi)$ of a pendulum for $\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ is correctly represented by

subject: Physics
q: An oscillator of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is at rest in its equilibrium position in a potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>–</mo><mi>X</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> comes from the right with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> and collides completely inelastic with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and sticks to it. This process repeats every time the oscillator crosses its equilibrium position. The amplitude of oscillations after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn></math> collisions is: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: An oscillator of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is at rest in its equilibrium position in a potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>–</mo><mi>X</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> comes from the right with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> and collides completely inelastic with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and sticks to it. This process repeats every time the oscillator crosses its equilibrium position. The amplitude of oscillations after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn></math> collisions is: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
In the first collision mu momentum will be imparted to the system. In the second collision when the momentum of (M + m) is in the opposite direction mu momentum of the particle will make its momentum zero. On 13th collision, 


Applying momentum conservation
mu=(M+13m)v

⇒v=muM+13m=u15 ⇒M=2m
⇒v= ωA

⇒u15= KM+13m×A

⇒A=115751=13

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $x=10 \sin \left(w t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) m$. The time period of motion is $3.14 \mathrm{~s}$. The velocity of the particle at $t=0$ is ______$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $x=10 \sin \left(w t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) m$. The time period of motion is $3.14 \mathrm{~s}$. The velocity of the particle at $t=0$ is ______$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Given,
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}=3.14=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega} \\
& \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \mathrm{x}=10 \sin \left(\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
& \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=10 \omega \cos \left(\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
& \text { at } \mathrm{t}=0 \\
& \mathrm{v}=10 \omega \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=10 \times 2 \times \frac{1}{2}[\mathrm{using} \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}] \\
& \mathrm{v}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two light identical springs of spring constant k are attached horizontally at the two ends of a uniform horizontal rod AB of length l and mass m. The rod is pivoted at its center 'O' and can rotate freely in horizontal plane. The other ends of the two springs are fixed to rigid supports as shown in figure. The rod is gently pushed through a small angle and released. The frequency of resulting oscillation is:
question: Two light identical springs of spring constant k are attached horizontally at the two ends of a uniform horizontal rod AB of length l and mass m. The rod is pivoted at its center 'O' and can rotate freely in horizontal plane. The other ends of the two springs are fixed to rigid supports as shown in figure. The rod is gently pushed through a small angle and released. The frequency of resulting oscillation is:
solution:
Torque on the rod about O,



τ=kx.l2×2

I α​=k.l2.θ.l2×2

I α​=kl22.θ

∴ ω2=kl22Ml212=6kM

f=12π6kM
